speaker: interviewer
text: Up and rolling again.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Elsbeth Lewin. January 4, 1996. Tape six. I'd like to ask you, Mrs. Lewin, as you go back in your mind, your experiences in Mainz as a child, the deterioration of life in Germany, did you-- did you think that you would survive that ordeal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- it never crossed my mind that I wouldn't. Let's put it that way. I think my parents may have felt differently. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think eventually the people who were stuck there during the war realized that they probably wouldn't make it. I had one aunt who came out through Spain and Portugal in 1990-- in 1941, which was a year after my mother and just before Pearl Harbor. And she took a-- she arrived-- she was the one who was supposed to go to, and who did go, to San Francisco. My mother, you know, arrived in San Francisco on the west coast and came here. Now, she came direct to-- to a-- to New York. And we picked her up on Staten Island [INAUDIBLE]. She was in a boat that wasn't any bigger than this house. It was-- I kept on thinking it was the the or the We went to pick her up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother burst into tears when she saw the boat. And my aunt, who was a very wonderful, matter-of-fact woman, and she said, what to do you want? Why you are crying? I'm here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she had all kinds of ventures-- of adventures coming here and coming to this country. And she had left her old mother in Germany. And old mother was relatively fortunate. She died of a heart attack just before they picked her up to go to Theresienstadt. But at that point, I think people felt that they might not survive. And, of course, my parents were very much aware of it. And I became aware of it. I mean, by that time I was getting to be old enough to know what was going on. And I was no longer in that sheltered atmosphere that I had been when I was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you to survive? What in your personality helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think that's a-- a-- a valid question. I-- I think I survived, because I was lucky enough to-- to have the opportunity to survive. I never had to put that kind of gumption that some people had to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, to be a forceful enough personality, or an optimistic enough personality, or a resourceful enough personality. And there are many, many wonderful people like that. I was never put to that test. Perhaps, I was fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: For anyone who will view this tape, for your children, for your grandchildren, for posterity, anyone who will see this in the future, is there a message that you would like to share with us, a message pertaining to the lessons we might-- might learn from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one was the one-- is the one that I mentioned before, participate in democracy. And I must say, I may be guilty. Maybe I'm not participating enough. But, you know, certainly, vote and-- and belong to the kind of organizations that you believe in, who will put-- put on the pressure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Another is that I'm grateful that I have had the opportunity to witness, to bear witness in-- in this manner. Because it's very important to remember the Holocaust, not to let the deniers get away with it, to remember what had gone before, that there was a normal life before and-- and the horrors of it, and never to let it happen again. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's basically, you know, we have to be very much on guard. And you never-- never can let up on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Lewin, thank you very much for sharing your life experience with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're most welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Frank Lewin, to whom I've been married since 1949. Happily, I might say. It's always an adventure. [LAUGHTER] And this is our youngest daughter, Miriam--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who is one of our delights. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: She's also the closest here, so we see  her often.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's very nice for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, as a couple. You've returned to Germany, to your homeland. I'd like to know your impressions as a couple returning to your-- to your home, the home of your childhood. I'm glad you said the home of our childhood, because it's not our homeland. Our homeland is the USA. We have returned several times, both separately and together. And I guess we were twice together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We went to Bayreuth twice. No, once, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went Bayreuth once. And we had another European trip. And we included Mainz. Unfortunately, we never got to Breslau. Maybe we'll get there some time. And it was always-- the times we were there together were always very quick, and almost perfunctory. We looked around. And, well, Frank didn't much care for it in the first place.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. To me, it wasn't a town that I was familiar with.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't mean anything. It didn't mean anything to him. And at the time, the first time we went back, I remember staying in a hotel which was very close to the last home I was-- we lived in. And it was full of beautiful pictures and oriental rugs. And I kept on saying to Frank, I wonder whom they took that from. And finally Frank said, you know, if you have that attitude about everything, we shouldn't be here. And so we, you know, we moved on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember this. That's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- so-- but, you know, there was that feeling. I've been there by myself once visiting Naomi when she was studying there. And we had a good time together. And the last time I was there was in '93, when the town of Mainz invited its former Jewish citizens. And that was a very interesting time. And I was impressed by the seriousness of our hosts, who wanted to be in touch with their former-- with the former community, and felt that they had lost something, which were-- they were trying to regain by interviewing us, inviting us, organizing various activities for us both serious and, and you know, just plain social. And-- and I have no quarrel with the present generation of Germans. I have always said, even when I was there in '75, when a lot of the people were still alive that were connected with the Nazis, I am not interested in talking to anyone who is older than I am. Anyone younger than I am, basically had nothing to do with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's been my feeling. And that is what my feeling about the present generation is. It wasn't their fault. It could have happened anywhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: If we hadn't been Jewish, God knows what we would have done and how we would have behaved. We certainly didn't care about the gypsies. We didn't even know about the gypsies, or the gays, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I feel-- I no longer feel-- I feel ambivalent about Germany as such. But I don't hate the Germans. I don't hate going there. It's just-- and it does have a special meaning for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the place where I was born. But it-- I don't have either feelings that it's my home or-- or that I hate them. I can be relatively neutral. I'm always interested in what happens there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to add anything to that, Professor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we really didn't have any different communal or joint feelings from what I described to you, except, as I said, that one time when I had this very strong feeling that I was home. I really did feel I was home when I was in this old town, the old part of Leipzig. Because it reminded me so very strongly of my youth, the buildings, the whole environment. And that's about the only strong feeling that I remember. Everything else I echo what Elsbeth said. I will go there for interests, especially musical interests. And I'll consider it a country. And I feel that it's language that I speak. So I can communicate much more easily than I could in France, for example. But I certainly have no feelings of antipathy towards the people there, except when they remind me, as I've said in my interview, of the Nazis, some little thing that will trigger me, a cap that somebody wears, or some subservient gesture that somebody makes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to turn my attention to Miriam now. Is there something that you would like to add, Miriam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we were growing up, we had very strange, ambiguous feelings about Germany, and about Germans, and about the German language. All-- I think all three of us. Because we knew Germany was where you were from. We that was where our family was from. We knew it was a wonderful culture. We knew it had wonderful music and-- and literature. And yet, the Germans were clearly the bad guys. And Germany had done bad things. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yet, in the '60s when we were growing up, Germany was our ally. Germany was our friend. And all of that was very confusing. I think for a long time, for all of us, until we-- until we were old enough to sort it out, intellectually. But still there's a confusion for me there. And when I've traveled in Germany, I have similar reactions to-- to the intonations of the language, to the sort of-- what you said-- the subservient nature. The sort of very formal nature, which I associate maybe through caricatures, or films, or I don't know what, with Nazis. But then I think, well, that's probably the way your families were, as good bourgeois Germans, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: He still bows that way. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's-- it's confusion that the children like us grew up with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that being the child of survivors-- because that is what your parents are-- has impacted you on the things that you did in your life or the way you think you were raised by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we never thought of ourselves as being children of survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Neither did we.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right, because we don't think of ourselves--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you don't think of yourselves as survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't-- we don't think ourselves as survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're witnesses. We're-- you know, we were there are at a crucial time. But we were not there at the crucial time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always said I was a first generation American. That was always how I identified myself. And I liked to say that I was a first generation American. I thought that was cool.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I thought it was kind of-- I thought it was interesting. It made me interesting, you know? [LAUGHTER] And I had these parents who were from Germany, which was interesting and who I sort of slowly realized had accents. I mean, I didn't realize that until I [LAUGHS] was walking home from school one day-- and the school is just down the street-- with a friend. And you drove by. And you yelled, see you later, or something like that. And my friend who I was walking with said, where's your mother from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, from see you later, where's your mother from? And I had-- I had never thought that-- I still don't think you have an accent. Daddy sometimes has-- has an accent, especially when he's nervous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially when I get excited.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he's giving a speech, you have an accent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. But-- and Oma and Opa, and Grammy and Grandpa certainly had accent.s

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in retrospect, I am-- sorry, I just hit the microphone. In retrospect, I am appalled at how much we made fun of their accents. Because I remember doing that a great deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't think they minded.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't mind that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They may not have even have heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHTER] And sort of the prime example of that is at the Seder, when Opa would always talk about the angle of the Lord, instead of the angel of the Lord. And year after year, we made fun of that. And now-- I was just talking to Eva about this. Eva and I were just talking on the phone about the Seder and how important that is. She said when she does the Seder, when she's at a Seder, she still hears it in Oma and Opa's voices, in Opa's voice, really, and in your voices. But she hears the ceremony with Opa's accent, the angle of the Lord.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's kind of nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: But all-- all the other things about, you know, every generation must act as though it were the generation that had come out of Egypt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we always stressed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We always stressed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You always stressed that. But it-- I mean, I'm glad you did. It also, in some ways, made our religion much more personal for us. Because it really was true. I mean, the real story of the Jews was-- was very personal for us. And that was another thing that we always said to our friends. If we invited friends to the Seder, which we liked to do, we always emphasized that to them, that it was true.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we explained it to them, to-- to non-Jews if they were-- that this really did happen to us. That Egypt was not some faraway place, but we came out of Egypt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, at Eva's Seders, she explains it to her children. I was at a Seder-- last year I was a Seder with them, without you guys. You were in California.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva explained it. Now, I hear it in Eva's voice, explaining to her children--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that her parents came--

speaker: interviewee
text: --about their grandparents coming out of Germany--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --coming out of Egypt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miriam, is there something that you would like to say to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: You say that in such a pregnant way, Marlene. [LAUGHTER] I-- I-- I have no great revelations. They-- they know how much we love them. I think it's-- I think it's amazing to have lived in one house for so long. I think it's such an un-American experience of this century that-- I wasn't born in this house. But I guess within a day or two, I came home to this house. My sisters were-- are a few years older than I am. We've lived in this house our whole lives. And I still think of this as home, which upsets my boyfriend when I-- whom I live with-- when I say, I'm going home to Princeton. He says, wait a minute. This is your home. [LAUGHTER] But in my mind, and in my dreams, and in my consciousness, and my unconsciousness, this is home. And it's been home for-- I'm 30-- I'm 38 in two days. It's my birthday. This has been my home for 38 years. That's amazing. It's-- I don't know whether you did it consciously or unconsciously, because you left your homes when you were teenagers. And you, I know, certainly Mom, I have a clear picture of your home. I have no picture of your home, Dad, I mean, in my mind. I have no pic--

speaker: interviewee
text: My home, you mean in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your home. Your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --life, Breslau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that kind of stability is very rare, even in America. But I do not feel-- I did not feel that kind of stability, obviously. Because as a teenager, I left Germany. Then I lived in Cuba. And then I lived in [INAUDIBLE]. And then I lived in New York, until I got married. And then I moved to  New Haven. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: But for us to have had this one home to come home to is amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been very conscious of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you've been very-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been very conscious of it. I mean, not-- I mean, at one point, we were thinking of moving to the west coast. I mean, I would have-- if you had had a real-- something solid, I mean, you would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I mentioned that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- I certainly wouldn't have refused to move. But I was very conscious about how-- how wonderful it is to have, you know, one-- one home. And, of course, we're-- I think we're exceptions. I mean, we've had one marriage, one home. I had one job in my life. I've had other jobs. But, you know, one job for 27 years or more. And you've had, more or less, the same experience. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, we are very fortunate.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're very-- we consider ourselves very fortunate. It's not that we're not adventurous. But we-- but we feel very stable.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're adventurous, not-- not in moving, or marriage, or personal relationships. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're fortunate to have you invite us into your home. And we thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you very much. You've been extraordinarily patient.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for taking the time to talk about your life experiences. And thank you, Miriam.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Marlene.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Abraham Blum of Nierstein. He is my great, great-grandfather-- No, he's my great-grandfather, my father's grandfather, on his mother's side. Probably taken in-- that picture's probably taken by a photographer in Mainz in the 1880's or '90s. And this is his wife, Carolina Blum nee Ross. She was his second wife, and my grandmother's mother also taken around the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house of Abraham Blum, Abraham Blum, in Nierstein. It actually was rebuilt to be a little store. He-- his-- one of his sons-in-law had a general store in there. So on the sign it says, Abraham Blum, [NON-ENGLISH], which means successor. And he was not in-- the son-in-law was in the dry goods business, not in the wine business. So he had this little dry goods store. And on the left is a monument to the dead in the War of 1870, '71 with France. I don't know if you can see it in the picture. And we have a letter off Abraham Blum to his oldest son in which he talks about the war, you know, affecting them, one way or the other. Carl Grunebaum is the son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the house of Abraham Blum's father, Nattan Blum in Nierstein, or near Nierstein, with-- with a little outhouse on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Get a wide shot of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Take the bottom. The top is not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it says, Wohnhaus, Nathan Blum, the house of Nathan Blum, called Geismauerhof, Nierstein [NON-ENGLISH], on the Rhine. Nierstein is on the Rhine, in-- south of Mainz in Rheinhessen. And it's famous for its wine. And it has the dates of Nattan Blum, my father's grandfather, 15th of April, 1796. Died the 3rd of March, 1877 and buried in Oppenheim on the Rhine, which was the next [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, Simon Gaertner, Simon Gertner, as a young man. I'm not sure when it was taken, but probably in the 1880's before-- either-- when he was first married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture taken at my grandparent's golden wedding, August 31, 1937. This is the second generation that I know of that had a golden wedding. My grand-- my grandfather's parents also had a golden wedding that was celebrated, in fact, by their son who emigrated to America. This was taken in Wiesbaden, Germany. And it shows, from left to right, myself, my father, Willi Gaertner, my grandmother, Juliane Gaertner, my grandfather, Simon Gaertner, and my mother, Hanna Gaertner. This is a picture in front of the house of Ferdinand Oppenheimer, whose name you can see written on the house. Ferdinand Oppenheimer was my mother's father. And he is in the picture. He is the gentleman all the way on the right. My grandmother is a lady in the middle, with her three children, Arnold Oppenheimer, Lucie Oppenheimer, and my mother, Hanna Oppenheimer. It must have been taken around 1900, maybe 1899, in Kulmbach, Germany. And the gentleman in the tall hat is my uncle from America, Uncle Adolph Oppenheimer, who saved so many of the family. And the lady in the doorway is their sister, Frieda. The other man I don't-- I can't identify.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Put this in perspective of the whole-- whole picture, and the whole street.

speaker: interviewee
text: The family next door is called Goldschmidt. And they-- many of them moved to England. But their-- the granddaughter is living, I think, in Cincinnati. I like that picture, because you can see here in front of the street where-- where they-- yeah. I mean, that's the continuation of that same street. And there she is with--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, know my cousin. Yeah, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. I know, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin is short and dumpy.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. My first impression was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at my grandmother, my mother's mother-- I think 67th, or 68th, or 69th birthday-- of herself and her five grandchildren. And in the first row is my cousin, Dr. [? Woody ?] Oppenheimer, in-- who lives in Santa Rosa, and then my grandmother. And on the other side of my grandmother is myself. In the back row is my cousin, Franz Oppenheimer, who lives in Washington D.C. My cousin, Lore Shaper, who lives in Point Richmond, California. And my cousin, Robert Sichel, who-- her brother, who lived in England, and died a few-- died some years ago, in-- I think in '83 of a heart attack. This is my grandmother, Betty Oppenheimer, in that same street where the picture was taken of-- of their house. It's in front of their house, but you can't see it. It is-- the street is called [INAUDIBLE]. And they lived at [INAUDIBLE]. And the tower is the [INAUDIBLE]. It is the-- literally, the-- the-- the gate to the-- to the-- to their little town. There was an upper gate and a lower gate. And my grandmother's right there in front of the house. Probably taken in the '30s-- yeah, it must have been taken in the '30s in Kulmbach Germany. This is the identification card of my mother, Hanna Oppenheimer-- that's what was her name maiden name-- taken during the First World War, then known as The Great War. She was a-- a volunteer nurse. And this was her picture in uniform probably taken in 1915. There's another one you'll love. --the one. This is a group of a truck-- truck drivers in the First World War, which is-- was my-- my father was in this group. And he's the one in the back row against the light thing there. And he was a [INAUDIBLE] is what they were called. And he learned how to drive in-- in-- in the war. And that was their mode of transportation. It was taken in Germany, I suppose. Though, it could have been on the western front in France someplace. And with his colleagues, and he is the fourth from the left in the back row.
